text: The patient is positioned supine for a laparoscopic mesh rectopexy. The laparoscopic trolley is on the left or leg end side, with the surgeon on the right and the camera operator on the left. The first assistant stands opposite the surgeon. Special instruments include bowel graspers, needle holders, and a polypropylene mesh. Trocar positions mirror those of a laparoscopic anterior resection, with a zero-degree 10 mm telescope at the umbilicus. The surgeon's right working ports are a 10 mm port in the right iliac fossa and a 5 mm port in the right upper abdomen. The assistant's 5 mm ports are in the left iliac fossa and left upper abdomen. During suturing, the surgeon may use the left abdominal 5 mm port for knotting. The rectum is retracted, and the peritoneum over the mesorectum is divided to access the pre-sacral space.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter from the right side and retracts it downwards, ensuring to remain in the avascular plane to avoid injury to the pelvic sympathetic nerves.

The surgeon performs further dissection in the pre-sacral space by dividing the peritoneal attachments of the rectum on the right side of the pelvis. The dissection continues posteriorly in the pre-sacral space towards the pelvic floor. The peritoneum on the left side of the pelvis is also divided, while preserving the lateral ligaments of the rectum. The dissection proceeds until the pelvic floor is reached, identified by the fibers of the levator ani muscle.

A six by four inches polypropylene mesh is placed in the pre-sacral space, covering the posterior wall of the rectum down to the pelvic floor. The mesh is positioned and fixed to the pre-sacral fascia with interrupted proline sutures in the midline. Additional seromuscular sutures are used to secure the mesh to both sides of the rectum.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum, ensuring the mesh is extraperitonealized to prevent contact with the small bowel. The mesh is not visible at the end of the procedure.
